enrichment.short_description: Open-source, low-code database framework from Germany for building custom CRM, DMS, ERP and groupware apps.
enrichment.seo_title: Limbas Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Limbas is a German open-source, low-code database framework for CRM, DMS and ERP apps. See features, GPL licensing, pricing and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Limbas is a low-code, open-source database application framework built by LIMBAS GmbH, a software company based in Oberhaching, Bavaria, Germany, that has been active in the open-source software space since 1996. Rather than being a single-purpose SaaS product, Limbas is a generalized database front-end and application-building platform: organizations use it as the foundation for custom CRM (customer relationship management), DMS (document management system), ERP, test-equipment management, groupware and CMS solutions. The core framework, often referred to as OpenLIMBAS, is released under the GNU General Public License version 2.0 and is hosted publicly on GitHub under the limbas/limbas repository, where it has accumulated a modest but active following of contributors and roughly a few dozen GitHub stars, reflecting its niche, enterprise-oriented positioning rather than a broad consumer developer audience. The codebase is primarily PHP with JavaScript, CSS and SCSS for the front end, and the application is designed to run on a Linux server with Apache and PHP 8 or later, connecting via PDO and unixODBC to a wide range of backend databases including PostgreSQL, MySQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle and SAP MaxDB or HANA. This database-agnostic architecture is one of Limbas's defining technical characteristics, allowing IT teams to build a unified application layer over heterogeneous data sources and to integrate with existing enterprise systems such as SAP. Because Limbas is a framework rather than a finished vertical product, its value proposition centers on flexibility and cost control: businesses that would otherwise pay for proprietary low-code platforms or commission bespoke software development can instead assemble tailored data-driven applications on top of Limbas's forms, workflow and reporting engine, then customize the resulting system without being locked into a single vendor's data model. LIMBAS GmbH monetizes this open-source core primarily through professional services, implementation consulting, and a commercially hosted variant marketed as LIMBAS live, which bundles managed hosting, support and additional enterprise features for organizations that prefer not to self-host. Pricing for the open-source, self-hosted edition is free under the GPL, while LIMBAS live and custom implementation work are quoted individually, following the pattern common to open-source-with-services business models. The target audience for Limbas skews toward mid-sized companies, larger corporations, and parts of the public sector, particularly within German-speaking Europe (the DACH region), where LIMBAS GmbH has built its customer relationships over nearly three decades of open-source project delivery. This is a meaningfully different audience from consumer-grade or Silicon Valley SaaS tools: Limbas customers are typically IT departments or internal operations teams that need a database-backed internal tool and have in-house PHP or low-code development capability, or who engage LIMBAS GmbH's consultants directly. In the competitive landscape, Limbas sits at an intersection of several categories. As a low-code application platform, it competes conceptually with commercial giants like Appian, OutSystems, Microsoft Power Apps and Zoho Creator, though at a much smaller scale and without their marketing budgets or cloud-native tooling. As an open-source, self-hostable database application builder, its closer peers are projects like Baserow, NocoDB and Budibase, all of which offer more modern, spreadsheet-like or visual builder interfaces and larger open-source communities; Limbas differentiates itself by offering a more mature, enterprise-database-oriented feature set (native ODBC connectivity to Oracle, MSSQL and SAP systems) rather than a lightweight no-code spreadsheet experience. When used specifically as a document management or CRM solution, Limbas also competes with dedicated open-source alternatives such as SuiteCRM, OpenKM and Mayan EDMS, though again Limbas's pitch is that a single flexible framework can cover CRM, DMS and custom workflow needs simultaneously rather than requiring separate specialized tools. Limbas has not raised venture capital and does not appear in typical startup funding databases; it operates as a privately held German GmbH funded through client services revenue, which aligns with its long operating history and steady, low-profile growth rather than the rapid-scaling trajectory typical of VC-backed SaaS companies. For a directory audience evaluating Limbas, the key considerations are that it requires PHP and Linux server administration skills to self-host, that documentation and community support are smaller and more German-centric than mainstream low-code platforms, and that its GPL licensing means the source is fully auditable and modifiable, which can be attractive to organizations with strict data sovereignty or on-premises requirements, particularly in regulated European industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Limbas is an open-source, low-code database application framework maintained by LIMBAS GmbH, a German software company based in Oberhaching, Bavaria, that has worked in the open-source space since 1996. Rather than shipping as a single finished product, Limbas provides a graphical database front-end and application-building layer that development teams use to construct custom CRM, DMS, ERP, groupware and CMS systems on top of existing enterprise databases.

The framework connects to a wide range of relational databases including PostgreSQL, MySQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle and SAP MaxDB or HANA, and is released under the GNU GPL v2.0, meaning the full source is available and self-hostable at no license cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Limbas offers a form and workflow builder for creating data-entry interfaces without extensive custom coding, ODBC and native connectors for integrating with SAP and other enterprise IT systems, and configurable modules that have been used to implement CRM, document management and test-equipment tracking systems.

Because it is framework-based rather than a fixed vertical application, Limbas is most valuable to organizations with in-house PHP or low-code development capacity, or those working with LIMBAS GmbH consultants, who can assemble a system tailored to specific internal processes rather than adapting to a rigid, pre-built product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core OpenLIMBAS framework is free to download and self-host under its GPL license. LIMBAS GmbH generates revenue through professional services, custom implementation, and a commercially hosted offering called LIMBAS live, which bundles managed hosting and support for organizations that prefer not to run the software themselves.

Because pricing for hosting and services is quoted per engagement, prospective users typically need to contact LIMBAS GmbH directly for a custom quote rather than selecting from published subscription tiers.

features.title: Low-code form and application builder
features.description: Build database-driven forms, views and workflows for CRM, DMS or ERP-style applications without writing a full custom application from scratch.

features.title: Multi-database connectivity
features.description: Connects via PDO and unixODBC to PostgreSQL, MySQL, MSSQL, Oracle and SAP MaxDB or HANA, letting IT teams unify access to heterogeneous enterprise data.

features.title: Document management (DMS) module
features.description: Provides document storage, versioning and retrieval capabilities that can be configured as a standalone DMS or combined with CRM data.

features.title: CRM and workflow configuration
features.description: Supports building customer relationship management and business-process workflows tailored to a company's specific operational needs.

features.title: SAP and enterprise IT integration
features.description: Native interfaces for connecting Limbas applications with SAP, ERP and groupware systems already in use at mid-sized and large organizations.

features.title: GPL v2 open-source licensing
features.description: Full source code is publicly available on GitHub, allowing self-hosting, auditing and modification without ongoing license fees.

features.title: Linux/Apache/PHP deployment
features.description: Runs on standard Linux server infrastructure with Apache and PHP 8 or later, fitting into existing on-premises IT environments.

features.title: LIMBAS live hosted option
features.description: A commercially supported, hosted version of the platform for organizations that want managed infrastructure and vendor support.

pros: Free, GPL-licensed core framework with no per-seat licensing costs for self-hosted use
Highly flexible: one framework can cover CRM, DMS, ERP and groupware needs
Native connectivity to enterprise databases including SAP MaxDB, Oracle and MSSQL
Nearly three decades of operating history and open-source project delivery experience
Full source code access supports data sovereignty and on-premises deployment requirements
cons: Requires PHP and Linux server administration skills to self-host and maintain
Small open-source community and GitHub following compared to modern low-code tools like Baserow or NocoDB
Documentation and community support skew toward German-speaking users
Dated interface conventions compared to newer visual, drag-and-drop app builders
Custom implementation work can require significant consulting engagement to get real value

faqs.question: Is Limbas free to use
faqs.answer: The core OpenLIMBAS framework is free and open source under the GNU GPL v2.0 license for self-hosted deployments. The commercially hosted LIMBAS live offering and professional services are priced separately.

faqs.question: What is Limbas used for
faqs.answer: Limbas is a low-code database framework used to build custom CRM, document management (DMS), ERP, groupware and CMS applications on top of existing enterprise databases.

faqs.question: What databases does Limbas support
faqs.answer: Limbas connects to PostgreSQL, MySQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle and SAP MaxDB or HANA via PDO and unixODBC.

faqs.question: Who develops Limbas
faqs.answer: Limbas is developed and maintained by LIMBAS GmbH, a German software company headquartered in Oberhaching, Bavaria, that has worked in the open-source software space since 1996.

faqs.question: Does Limbas require coding knowledge
faqs.answer: Limbas is a low-code platform, so many tasks can be done through its form and workflow builder, but self-hosting and deeper customization generally require PHP and Linux server administration skills.

faqs.question: How does Limbas compare to Baserow or NocoDB
faqs.answer: Limbas offers deeper native connectivity to enterprise databases like SAP MaxDB and Oracle, while Baserow and NocoDB provide more modern, spreadsheet-style interfaces and larger open-source communities.

use_cases.title: Internal CRM for mid-sized companies
use_cases.description: Companies build a tailored customer relationship management system on Limbas that reflects their specific sales process rather than adapting to a rigid commercial CRM.
use_cases.audience: Mid-sized businesses with in-house IT or PHP development capacity

use_cases.title: Document management for regulated industries
use_cases.description: Organizations use the Limbas DMS module to store, version and retrieve documents on-premises, supporting data sovereignty requirements.
use_cases.audience: Public sector and regulated European organizations

use_cases.title: Custom database front-end over SAP and legacy systems
use_cases.description: IT teams use Limbas to build a unified application layer over Oracle, MSSQL or SAP MaxDB databases already in production.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT departments integrating multiple legacy systems

enrichment.short_description: Open-source online survey software offering a free self-hosted edition and a paid cloud-hosted version.
enrichment.seo_title: LimeSurvey Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LimeSurvey is an open-source survey tool with a free self-hosted edition and paid cloud plans from 29 euros per month. Compare features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LimeSurvey is one of the longest-running open-source survey platforms on the web, tracing its origins to a SourceForge project called PHPSurveyor, launched on February 20, 2003 by Australian software developer Jason Cleeland, with its first public release (version 0.93) following on March 5, 2003. The project gained early real-world visibility when it was used during the 2004 U.S. presidential election to help track and report voting irregularities, reportedly logging over 13,500 incidents within the first ten hours of voting, an early demonstration of the tool's capacity for high-volume, time-sensitive data collection. On May 17, 2007, the project was renamed from PHPSurveyor to LimeSurvey specifically to simplify software licensing by removing the ambiguous reference to PHP from the name. The software underwent a complete architectural rewrite in 2012, moving to an MVC (model-view-controller) structure built on the Yii PHP framework, and later adopted the Twig templating engine with the version 3.0 release in December 2017. To formalize commercial stewardship of the increasingly popular open-source project, LimeSurvey GmbH was founded in August 2015 as a limited liability company headquartered in Hamburg, Germany, co-founded by long-time project leader Carsten Schmitz, who continues to lead the company. LimeSurvey GmbH today operates with a lean team of roughly 18 to 20 employees, reflecting an efficient, services-and-subscription-driven business model rather than venture-backed rapid scaling; public reporting has cited annual revenue in the low single-digit millions of dollars. LimeSurvey's architecture remains PHP-based and database-agnostic, supporting MySQL, SQLite, PostgreSQL or Microsoft SQL Server as a backend, and the survey engine itself is localized into 116 languages and dialects, making it one of the most linguistically comprehensive survey tools available, open source or commercial. The product is offered in two primary forms. The Community Edition is the free, GPL-licensed, self-hosted version that any organization or individual can download, install and run on their own server infrastructure with no feature restrictions tied to payment, only the requirement of self-managed hosting and community-based support. The second form, LimeSurvey Cloud, is a fully hosted SaaS offering, comparable in convenience to commercial competitors like SurveyMonkey or Typeform, but priced differently: rather than gating features behind a paywall, LimeSurvey Cloud's pricing scales primarily with the number of survey responses collected per period, which the company positions as a fairer model for organizations with unpredictable but occasionally high response volumes. As of the most recent published pricing, LimeSurvey Cloud offers a free entry tier with a limited number of monthly responses, followed by four paid plans: Basic at approximately 34 euros per month for one admin user, 1,000 responses per month and 250 MB of storage; Expert at approximately 29 euros per month when billed annually (348 euros per year), supporting up to three admin users, 10,000 responses per year, 1 GB of storage, white-label branding and email support; Business at approximately 75 euros per month billed annually (900 euros per year), supporting 5 to 10 admin users, 50,000 responses per year, 3 GB of storage, an alias domain and priority support; and Corporate, a custom-quoted tier for organizations needing SSO via LDAP or SAML, dedicated servers and VIP support. LimeSurvey also offers discretionary discounts of 30 percent for teachers and charitable organizations and 50 percent for students, and customers can choose among seven hosting regions including Germany, Finland, the UK, the US, Canada and Australia, which is a meaningful differentiator for organizations with GDPR or data-residency requirements. LimeSurvey's target audience spans academic researchers, market researchers, public sector agencies, nonprofits and businesses that need advanced survey logic (branching, quotas, randomization, multilingual surveys) without the recurring per-response fees of commercial SaaS survey tools, or that specifically require an on-premises, auditable, self-hosted deployment for compliance reasons. Its principal competitors include SurveyMonkey, Typeform, Qualtrics, Google Forms and Alchemer (formerly SurveyGizmo); LimeSurvey differentiates itself from all of these primarily through its open-source licensing and self-hosting option, its response-based rather than feature-gated cloud pricing, and its unusually deep localization into over a hundred languages, which makes it a common choice for international research consortia, universities and multilingual public-sector surveys where commercial alternatives either lack language coverage or charge a premium for it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LimeSurvey began life in 2003 as PHPSurveyor, an open-source survey tool created by Australian developer Jason Cleeland, and was renamed LimeSurvey in 2007. LimeSurvey GmbH, the company that now stewards the project, was formally founded in August 2015 in Hamburg, Germany, and is led by long-time project leader Carsten Schmitz.

The software is available as a free, GPL-licensed, self-hosted Community Edition, and as LimeSurvey Cloud, a paid hosted service whose pricing is based on survey response volume rather than feature restrictions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LimeSurvey supports unlimited surveys and questions, advanced survey logic such as branching and quotas, and localization into 116 languages, making it one of the most internationally capable survey platforms available.

The platform runs on PHP with the Yii framework and Twig templating, supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite or MSSQL as a database backend, and offers white-label branding, custom domains, and SSO options (LDAP, SAML) on higher cloud tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LimeSurvey Cloud offers a free entry tier with limited monthly responses, then scales through Basic (about 34 euros per month), Expert (about 29 euros per month billed annually), Business (about 75 euros per month billed annually) and a custom-quoted Corporate tier with SSO and dedicated hosting.

The self-hosted Community Edition remains free under the GPL license for organizations able to manage their own server infrastructure, with discounts available for teachers, students and nonprofits on the Cloud plans.

features.title: Free, self-hosted Community Edition
features.description: Fully open-source, GPL-licensed survey software that can be downloaded and run on any PHP-compatible server at no license cost.

features.title: LimeSurvey Cloud hosting
features.description: A managed SaaS version priced by response volume rather than gated features, available across seven hosting regions including the EU, UK, US, Canada and Australia.

features.title: Advanced survey logic
features.description: Supports branching, quotas, randomization and conditional question logic for complex research and market-research surveys.

features.title: Extensive localization
features.description: Survey interfaces and templates are available in 116 languages and dialects, supporting multilingual and international research projects.

features.title: White-label and custom branding
features.description: Available on Expert tier and above, allowing organizations to present surveys under their own domain and visual identity.

features.title: SSO and enterprise access control
features.description: LDAP and SAML single sign-on are available on the Corporate tier for organizations needing centralized authentication.

features.title: REST API and integrations
features.description: Programmatic access for embedding surveys, exporting data and integrating with other research or CRM systems.

features.title: Data residency options
features.description: Seven hosting regions let organizations keep survey data within specific jurisdictions for GDPR or compliance purposes.

pros: Free, fully open-source self-hosted option with no feature restrictions
Response-based cloud pricing can be more predictable than per-seat SaaS competitors
Exceptional localization with 116 supported languages
Long operating history (since 2003) and mature, stable codebase
Multiple hosting regions support GDPR and data-residency needs
cons: Self-hosting requires PHP server administration knowledge and ongoing maintenance
Interface feels less modern than newer competitors like Typeform
Cloud pricing based on response volume can become costly for very high-traffic surveys
Small company size (under 25 employees) means comparatively limited support capacity
Advanced features like white-labeling and SSO are locked behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: Is LimeSurvey free
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted Community Edition is free and open source under the GPL license. LimeSurvey Cloud, the hosted version, has a limited free tier and then paid plans starting around 29 to 34 euros per month.

faqs.question: Who owns LimeSurvey
faqs.answer: LimeSurvey is developed and commercially operated by LimeSurvey GmbH, a company founded in August 2015 and headquartered in Hamburg, Germany, led by Carsten Schmitz.

faqs.question: How is LimeSurvey Cloud priced
faqs.answer: LimeSurvey Cloud pricing is based primarily on the number of survey responses collected per month or year, rather than on locking features behind higher tiers, with plans ranging from a free tier to a custom-quoted Corporate plan.

faqs.question: Can I self-host LimeSurvey
faqs.answer: Yes, LimeSurvey's Community Edition can be downloaded and self-hosted on any server running PHP with a supported database such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite or MSSQL.

faqs.question: How many languages does LimeSurvey support
faqs.answer: LimeSurvey is localized into 116 languages and dialects, making it one of the most internationally capable open-source survey tools.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LimeSurvey and SurveyMonkey
faqs.answer: LimeSurvey offers a free, open-source self-hosted option and response-based cloud pricing, while SurveyMonkey is a closed-source commercial SaaS tool with feature-gated subscription tiers.

use_cases.title: Academic and market research
use_cases.description: Universities and research teams use LimeSurvey's advanced logic, quotas and multilingual support to run complex studies.
use_cases.audience: Academic researchers and market research firms

use_cases.title: Public sector and nonprofit surveys
use_cases.description: Organizations self-host LimeSurvey to maintain full data control and avoid per-response SaaS fees, aided by nonprofit discounts.

use_cases.title: Multilingual international surveys
use_cases.description: Companies and NGOs run surveys across many countries using LimeSurvey's 116-language localization.

enrichment.short_description: No-code AI agent platform that builds automated AI teammates for email, scheduling, meetings and support.
enrichment.seo_title: Lindy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lindy is a no-code AI agent builder for automating email, scheduling and support. See Lindy pricing plans from 49.99 dollars per month, features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lindy is a no-code AI agent building platform founded in 2023 by Flo Crivello and headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company positions its product around the concept of building autonomous AI teammates, referred to internally as Lindies, that can be assigned repetitive business tasks such as managing email inboxes, drafting responses in a user's own voice, scheduling and rescheduling meetings, joining video calls to take notes and extract action items, qualifying inbound leads, handling customer support tickets, conducting research, and executing multi-step workflows across connected business tools. Lindy raised a Series A funding round of approximately 50 million dollars led by Battery Ventures, with additional participation from a16z (Andreessen Horowitz), Menlo Ventures, Coatue and Tiger Global, as well as prominent angel investors including Elad Gil and Jack Altman, giving the company substantial capital to compete in the fast-moving AI agent and workflow automation category. As of 2026, Lindy employs roughly 37 people, a lean headcount relative to its funding, and public revenue estimates place the company at approximately 5.1 million dollars in annual revenue for 2024, reflecting rapid early-stage growth typical of a well-funded AI-native startup. Architecturally, Lindy differentiates itself from generic large language model chat interfaces by embedding a built-in approval workflow: rather than allowing an agent to act fully autonomously and irreversibly, Lindy's design has agents draft actions such as emails or messages for explicit user review and approval before they are sent, a deliberate product decision aimed at building user trust in agentic automation. The platform connects to over 100 third-party tools and services, including Gmail, Outlook, Slack, Notion, HubSpot, Salesforce, Microsoft Teams and Zoom, and it also offers direct iMessage and SMS access, letting users interact with their AI agents from a native messaging interface around the clock. Lindy markets itself with a privacy-first stance, stating that customer data is not sold or used to train underlying models without consent, an increasingly important differentiator as AI automation tools handle sensitive business communications. Pricing follows a tiered subscription model without a persistent free plan, though every paid tier includes a 7-day free trial that does not require a credit card. The entry-level Plus plan costs 49.99 dollars per month and supports up to two connected inboxes with standard usage limits, aimed at individual users just getting started with AI agents. The Pro plan, at 99.99 dollars per month, offers roughly three times the usage allowance of Plus, supports up to three inboxes, and crucially adds computer use capability, meaning the agent can control a browser to complete tasks that require navigating web interfaces directly rather than relying solely on API integrations. The Max plan, at 199.99 dollars per month, offers roughly seven times the Plus usage allowance and up to five inboxes, targeted at power users with heavy day-to-day automation workloads, though it does not add further capabilities beyond scale. At the top of the pricing structure, Lindy offers a custom-quoted Enterprise plan for organizations that require single sign-on (SSO), SCIM provisioning for automated user management, HIPAA compliance for handling protected health information, detailed audit logs, a signed business associate agreement (BAA), and dedicated onboarding and support, positioning Lindy as usable in regulated industries such as healthcare in addition to general business automation. Lindy's target audience spans two distinct segments: individual professionals, freelancers and small teams who want to automate personal productivity tasks like inbox triage and meeting notes without engineering resources, and larger organizations building customer-facing or internal automation at scale, such as automated lead qualification or support ticket handling, who need the enterprise security and compliance features. In the increasingly crowded AI agent and workflow automation market, Lindy's most direct competitors include Zapier, which has expanded from simple app-to-app automation into AI-powered agent workflows through products like Zapier Agents, Make (formerly Integromat), n8n, an open-source workflow automation tool popular among technical users, Gumloop, Relay.app, Bardeen, and a growing set of AI-native agent orchestration platforms. Lindy differentiates itself from pure workflow automation tools like Zapier or Make by leaning into a conversational, assistant-like product framing, where users describe what they want a Lindy to do in natural language rather than manually wiring together trigger-and-action flowcharts, and by emphasizing built-in approval gates that keep a human in the loop for consequential actions, addressing a common concern about handing fully autonomous control to AI agents in production business contexts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lindy is a no-code AI agent platform founded in 2023 by Flo Crivello and based in San Francisco. The company has raised approximately 50 million dollars in Series A funding led by Battery Ventures, with backing from a16z, Menlo Ventures, Coatue and Tiger Global.

Lindy lets users build AI agents, called Lindies, that automate tasks like email management, scheduling, meeting notes, lead qualification and customer support, connecting to over 100 business tools including Gmail, Slack, Notion, HubSpot and Salesforce.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include natural-language agent creation, an approval workflow where agents draft actions for human review before sending, meeting joining and note-taking with action-item extraction, and iMessage/SMS access for interacting with agents outside a web browser.

Higher tiers add computer use, which lets an agent control a web browser directly to complete tasks that lack a native API integration, along with enterprise-grade security features like SSO, SCIM and HIPAA compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lindy offers three self-serve monthly plans: Plus at 49.99 dollars, Pro at 99.99 dollars, and Max at 199.99 dollars, each scaling usage limits and the number of connected inboxes, with Pro and above adding browser-based computer use.
A custom-quoted Enterprise plan adds SSO, SCIM provisioning, HIPAA compliance, audit logs and a signed BAA. All individual plans include a 7-day free trial with no credit card required.

features.title: No-code AI agent builder
features.description: Create autonomous AI agents (Lindies) using natural-language instructions rather than technical workflow diagrams.

features.title: Built-in approval workflow
features.description: Agents draft emails and actions for explicit human review and approval before anything is sent, keeping a human in the loop.

features.title: Meeting notes and action items
features.description: Lindy can join video calls, record and transcribe meetings, and automatically extract action items and summaries.

features.title: 100+ integrations
features.description: Connects to Gmail, Outlook, Slack, Notion, HubSpot, Salesforce, Microsoft Teams, Zoom and dozens of other business tools.

features.title: Computer use (browser automation)
features.description: Available on Pro tier and above, letting agents control a web browser to complete tasks without a native API integration.

features.title: iMessage and SMS access
features.description: Users can interact with their AI agents directly via text message or iMessage around the clock.

features.title: Enterprise security and compliance
features.description: Enterprise plan includes SSO, SCIM provisioning, HIPAA compliance, audit logs and a signed business associate agreement.

features.title: Privacy-first data handling
features.description: Lindy states customer data is not sold or used to train underlying models without consent.

pros: Natural-language agent creation requires no coding or technical workflow design
Built-in human approval step reduces risk of fully autonomous AI mistakes
Broad integration library covering the most common business tools
Well-funded startup (50 million dollars raised) with strong investor backing
Enterprise tier supports regulated industries via HIPAA compliance and audit logs
cons: No persistent free tier, only a 7-day trial before payment is required
Higher tiers (Pro and Max) needed to unlock browser-based computer use and heavier usage
Relatively young company (founded 2023) with a limited multi-year track record
Enterprise pricing is custom and not published, requiring a sales conversation
Usage-based limits across plans can be difficult to predict for high-volume workflows

faqs.question: What is Lindy used for
faqs.answer: Lindy is used to build no-code AI agents that automate business tasks such as email management, meeting scheduling, note-taking, lead qualification and customer support.

faqs.question: How much does Lindy cost
faqs.answer: Lindy's Plus plan starts at 49.99 dollars per month, with Pro at 99.99 dollars and Max at 199.99 dollars per month. An Enterprise plan with custom pricing is available for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded Lindy
faqs.answer: Lindy was founded in 2023 by Flo Crivello and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Lindy have a free plan
faqs.answer: Lindy does not offer a permanent free plan, but all individual paid plans include a 7-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: What is computer use in Lindy
faqs.answer: Computer use is a feature available on Lindy's Pro plan and above that allows an AI agent to control a web browser directly to complete tasks that do not have a native API integration.

faqs.question: Is Lindy suitable for regulated industries like healthcare
faqs.answer: Lindy's Enterprise plan includes HIPAA compliance, audit logs and a signed business associate agreement, making it usable for healthcare-related workflows that involve protected health information.

use_cases.title: Inbox and calendar automation
use_cases.description: Individual professionals use Lindy to triage email, draft responses in their own voice and manage scheduling automatically.

use_cases.title: Sales lead qualification
use_cases.description: Sales teams deploy Lindy agents to qualify inbound leads and route them appropriately before a human rep engages.

use_cases.title: Automated customer support triage
use_cases.description: Support teams use Lindy to handle first-line ticket responses and escalate complex issues to human agents.
use_cases.audience: Customer support teams at growing companies

enrichment.short_description: Fast, keyboard-driven issue tracking and project management tool built for software engineering teams.
enrichment.company_size: Roughly 100-250 employees (2026, estimates vary by source)
enrichment.seo_title: Linear Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Linear is a fast issue tracking tool for software teams, valued at 1.25 billion dollars. Compare Linear pricing, features and alternatives like Jira and Asana.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Linear is a project and issue tracking platform purpose-built for software development teams, founded in 2019 by Karri Saarinen (CEO), Tuomas Artman (CTO) and Jori Lallo, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Saarinen previously worked as a design lead at Airbnb and Coinbase, while Artman brought engineering leadership experience from Uber, and the founding team's product design pedigree is reflected heavily in Linear's identity: the company built its reputation on speed, keyboard-first navigation, and an opinionated, minimalist interface that stands in deliberate contrast to older, more configurable but heavier tools like Jira. Linear's funding history reflects strong and sustained investor confidence in this positioning. The company raised a 4.2 million dollar seed round led by Sequoia Capital in November 2019, followed by a 13 million dollar Series A also from Sequoia in December 2020, a 35 million dollar Series B led by Accel in September 2023, and most recently an 82 million dollar Series C led by Accel in June 2025, which valued the company at 1.25 billion dollars, officially making Linear a unicorn. Total funding raised across all rounds stands at approximately 134.2 million dollars, with Sequoia Capital and Accel serving as the primary institutional backers throughout the company's growth, alongside other participants including 01 Advisors, Seven Seven Six, Designer Fund and Index Ventures. As of 2026, employee count estimates vary meaningfully by data source, ranging from roughly 118 to 234 people, reflecting the imprecision common in third-party headcount tracking for private companies, but Linear is broadly understood to operate with a lean, product-focused team relative to its valuation and customer base. The company states that it serves more than 33,000 organizations, including notable customers such as OpenAI, Coinbase and Ramp, positioning Linear firmly as the default issue tracker for many venture-backed, engineering-driven technology companies. Architecturally and philosophically, Linear is built around what the company calls the Linear Method, a set of opinionated best practices for software development workflows that emphasizes short, focused work cycles, clear ownership of issues, and minimizing process overhead; this is reflected directly in product features like Cycles (Linear's version of sprints), Triage (a queue for incoming issues that need classification), and a roadmap and project tracking layer that connects individual issues to broader initiatives. The product is known for its speed and responsiveness, built with a local-first architecture that allows near-instant interactions even with large issue databases, and for extensive keyboard shortcuts that let experienced users navigate and manage issues without touching a mouse. Linear's pricing follows a four-tier freemium model. The Free plan costs nothing, supports unlimited members, and allows up to 2 teams and 250 issues, along with access to Linear's agent platform, making it viable for small teams or early-stage startups. The Basic plan costs 10 dollars per user per month when billed yearly and adds unlimited issues, support for up to 5 teams, unlimited file uploads and admin roles. The Business plan, at 16 dollars per user per month billed yearly, is the most feature-rich self-serve tier, adding unlimited teams, private teams and guest accounts, Triage Intelligence (AI-assisted issue triage), Linear Agent automations (in beta), Code Intelligence (in beta, connecting issues to code changes), Linear Insights analytics, Linear Asks, and native integrations with Zendesk and Intercom. At the top, an Enterprise plan offers custom, annual-only pricing with invoice and purchase-order billing, SAML and SCIM support for identity management, granular admin controls, enterprise-grade security certifications, and dedicated migration and onboarding support with a named account manager. Linear has increasingly layered AI capabilities into its product, including AI-assisted triage and coding-session features that draw on separate usage-based credits, reflecting the broader industry shift toward embedding AI agents directly into developer workflow tools. Linear's target audience is squarely software engineering and product teams at technology companies, particularly those that value design quality, speed and a curated feature set over Jira's extensive but often overwhelming configurability. Its main competitors include Atlassian's Jira, the long-standing incumbent in enterprise issue tracking, as well as Asana, ClickUp, Monday.com, Height, Shortcut and Trello. Linear differentiates itself from this field primarily through its uncompromising focus on speed and interface craft, its opinionated rather than infinitely configurable workflow model, and its strong brand affinity among high-growth, well-funded startups and engineering-culture-forward companies, a positioning reinforced by its billion-dollar-plus valuation and backing from top-tier venture firms like Sequoia and Accel.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Linear was founded in 2019 by Karri Saarinen, Tuomas Artman and Jori Lallo, and is headquartered in San Francisco. The company has raised roughly 134.2 million dollars in total funding, most recently an 82 million dollar Series C led by Accel in June 2025 at a 1.25 billion dollar valuation.

Linear is used by more than 33,000 companies, including OpenAI, Coinbase and Ramp, and is built around the Linear Method, an opinionated approach to software development workflow that emphasizes speed, focus and minimal process overhead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core product features include Cycles for time-boxed sprints, a Triage queue for incoming issues, project and roadmap tracking, and an extensive keyboard-shortcut-driven interface built for speed.
Higher tiers add AI-assisted Triage Intelligence, beta Linear Agent automations and Code Intelligence, Linear Insights analytics, and integrations with tools like Zendesk and Intercom.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Linear offers a free plan (unlimited members, 2 teams, 250 issues), a Basic plan at 10 dollars per user per month billed yearly, and a Business plan at 16 dollars per user per month billed yearly with AI and automation features.
An Enterprise plan offers custom, annual-only pricing with SAML/SCIM, invoice billing and dedicated support for larger organizations.

Generous free tier supports small teams with unlimited members
Strong backing from Sequoia and Accel with a 1.25 billion dollar valuation signaling long-term stability

cons: Free plan caps out at 250 issues and 2 teams, requiring an upgrade for growing teams

Opinionated workflow model may not suit teams outside software engineering

faqs.answer: Linear focuses on speed, a minimalist interface and an opinionated workflow, while Jira offers extensive configurability and a broader feature set aimed at larger, more complex organizations.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archive with tags, lists, dead-link monitoring and Internet Archive backup.
enrichment.seo_title: LinkAce Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LinkAce is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archive with tags, lists and Internet Archive backup. See features, setup requirements and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LinkAce is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archiving application created by German developer Kevin Woblick, known online as Kovah, who released the first stable version, LinkAce 1.0, on December 15, 2020, after more than a year of development. The project is distributed under the GPL-3.0 license and is hosted publicly on GitHub under Kovah/LinkAce, where it has grown to roughly 3,300 stars and around 200 forks as of 2026, with active ongoing releases (the project reached version 2.6.0 in July 2026 across 129 total tagged releases), indicating a still-maintained, single-maintainer-led open-source project rather than a venture-backed company. LinkAce is built on the Laravel PHP framework with Blade templating, and it is designed to be deployed primarily via Docker, with beta support for Kubernetes deployments for users running larger self-hosted infrastructure. Functionally, LinkAce positions itself as a personal or team bookmark archive rather than a simple link-saving tool: beyond basic tagging, it organizes bookmarks into custom lists for grouping links by topic or occasion, and it actively monitors the availability of saved links, notifying users when a site has moved or gone offline. Every saved link is automatically submitted to the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine for long-term preservation, which is one of LinkAce's most distinctive features relative to lighter-weight bookmark tools, since it protects against link rot even if the original site disappears entirely. LinkAce supports multi-user deployments with internal sharing controls, and it offers single sign-on through both OAuth and OIDC providers, making it suitable for small teams or organizations that want centralized authentication rather than separate LinkAce credentials. The application exposes a full REST API covering all of its features, enabling programmatic access and custom integrations, and it connects to Zapier, which the project describes as unlocking compatibility with several thousand other applications including Google Drive, Discord and Slack, extending LinkAce's utility well beyond a standalone bookmarking app into broader personal or team automation stacks. Additional features include a browser bookmarklet for quick saving from any page, automatic title and description generation when a link is added, light and dark theme options, private and public link sharing controls, RSS feed support for sharing curated collections, HTML import and export for migrating bookmarks to and from browsers or other bookmark managers, and AWS S3-compatible backup support for safeguarding archived data. LinkAce's interface and documentation are available in seven languages: English, German, French, Japanese, Spanish, Polish and Chinese, reflecting an internationally distributed user base typical of popular self-hosted open-source software. Because LinkAce is entirely free and open source with no commercial company or paid tier behind it, it is typically run on personal home servers, small business infrastructure, or self-hosting platforms like Unraid, Synology NAS or generic VPS providers, and its ongoing development is sustained through community contributions and, presumably, developer goodwill or sponsorship rather than product revenue. LinkAce's target audience includes privacy-conscious individuals who want full control over their bookmark data rather than trusting a third-party cloud service, self-hosting hobbyists building out a personal application stack (a community often associated with tools cataloged on sites like awesome-selfhosted), researchers and knowledge workers who need durable archival of reference links rather than ephemeral bookmarks, and small teams that want a shared, searchable link library with access control. Within the self-hosted bookmark manager category, LinkAce's closest and most frequently compared competitor is linkding, a similarly popular open-source bookmark manager built on Django and Python rather than Laravel and PHP; the two projects are commonly discussed together in self-hosting communities, with LinkAce generally seen as offering a richer built-in feature set (multi-provider SSO, list-and-tag hybrid organization, native Zapier integration) at the cost of a heavier PHP/Laravel stack, while linkding is generally regarded as more minimal and faster to set up. Other adjacent competitors include Shaarli, a lightweight PHP bookmarking tool, Wallabag, a read-it-later and archiving tool, and commercial cloud alternatives like Raindrop.io and Pocket (the latter of which was shut down by Mozilla in 2025), which LinkAce and similar self-hosted tools have increasingly positioned themselves as durable, privacy-respecting replacements for.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LinkAce is a free, open-source, self-hosted bookmark archiving tool created by German developer Kevin Woblick (Kovah), with its first stable release, version 1.0, launched on December 15, 2020. It is licensed under GPL-3.0 and built on the Laravel PHP framework, distributed primarily as a Docker deployment.
The project has no associated company; it is maintained as a community open-source project on GitHub, where it has accumulated roughly 3,300 stars, with active releases continuing through 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LinkAce organizes bookmarks with both tags and custom lists, monitors saved links for availability, and automatically archives every saved page to the Internet Archive for long-term preservation against link rot.

It supports multi-user access with OAuth and OIDC single sign-on, exposes a full REST API, integrates with Zapier for connecting to thousands of other apps, and offers HTML import/export and S3-compatible backups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LinkAce is completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tier or hosted commercial version; users are responsible for their own server or Docker hosting infrastructure.
Because there is no company behind LinkAce, ongoing development relies on community contributions and voluntary maintenance rather than subscription revenue.

features.title: Multi-user support with SSO

features.title: REST API and Zapier integration
features.description: Full REST API for custom integrations, plus native Zapier support connecting to thousands of other applications.

features.title: Docker and Kubernetes deployment
features.description: Ships with pre-configured Docker setups and a web installer, with beta support for Kubernetes deployments.

features.title: Multilingual interface
features.description: Available in English, German, French, Japanese, Spanish, Polish and Chinese.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tier or vendor lock-in
Automatic Internet Archive backup protects against link rot uniquely well among bookmark managers
Rich built-in feature set including multi-provider SSO and Zapier integration
Full REST API supports deep custom integrations
Active, ongoing development with regular releases as of 2026
cons: Requires self-hosting via Docker, which demands basic server administration skills
Smaller community (about 3,300 GitHub stars) than close competitor linkding
Heavier Laravel/PHP stack compared to more minimal self-hosted alternatives
No official hosted/cloud version for non-technical users
Maintained largely by a single core developer, creating bus-factor risk

faqs.question: Is LinkAce free
faqs.answer: Yes, LinkAce is completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers or hosted commercial version.

faqs.question: Who created LinkAce
faqs.answer: LinkAce was created by German developer Kevin Woblick, known online as Kovah, who released version 1.0 on December 15, 2020.

faqs.question: How is LinkAce different from linkding
faqs.answer: LinkAce is built on Laravel/PHP and offers a richer built-in feature set including multi-provider SSO and native Zapier integration, while linkding is built on Django/Python and is generally considered more minimal and lightweight, with a larger GitHub community.

faqs.question: Does LinkAce back up bookmarks automatically
faqs.answer: Yes, every link saved in LinkAce is automatically submitted to the Internet Archive Wayback Machine, protecting against link rot if the original page goes offline.

faqs.question: How do I install LinkAce
faqs.answer: LinkAce is designed to be deployed via Docker using pre-configured setups and a built-in web installer, with beta support for Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Does LinkAce support multiple users
faqs.answer: Yes, LinkAce supports multi-user deployments with internal sharing and single sign-on via OAuth or OIDC providers.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted privacy-focused bookmarking
